title: Perimenopause Decoded: What Every Woman Over 35 Needs to Know
guestBio: Dr. Maria Ines 'Mari' Mitrani is a physician-scientist specializing in regenerative and preventive medicine, with deep expertise in stem cell research and women's health innovation. She has co-founded multiple biotech companies spanning stem cells, exosomes, genetic testing, and metabolic health, and was recognized by IFAH as one of the Top 100 Healthcare Leaders. Dr. Mari is a passionate advocate for women, minorities, animals, and the environment, and brings both scientific rigor and personal experience to her work in perimenopause and longevity.
excerpt: If you've hit your mid-thirties and something feels off — the brain fog, the mood shifts, the sense that you've lost yourself — this conversation is the one you didn't know you needed. Host Aimee Rickabus sits down with regenerative medicine pioneer Dr. Mari Mitrani to decode perimenopause with honesty, science, and zero shame. This isn't an ending. It's a beginning — and this episode will change how you see it.
metaDescription: Dr. Mari Mitrani joins The Manage Her to decode perimenopause symptoms, HRT myths, stem cells, and how to advocate for your health after 35.
keyTakeaways: Perimenopause is a transition, not a breakdown — you are not broken, and what you are experiencing is real and valid.
Women were excluded from clinical research until 1993, which means decades of medical guidance was built without us in mind, and self-advocacy is now a non-negotiable skill.

The Women's Health Initiative study that scared a generation of women away from estrogen had serious methodological flaws, and personalized, lower-dose hormone care looks very different from what that study tested.

Symptoms like brain fog, mood changes, joint pain, and the feeling of 'I don't feel like myself' are recognized perimenopause signs — start tracking your health from age 35, not just when hot flashes arrive.
Muscle and bone density are longevity tools, not vanity goals — strength training in midlife is one of the most powerful investments you can make in your future self.
Your ethnicity can influence how long perimenopause lasts and how it presents, which is why personalized care from a trained provider matters more than generic advice.
Stem cells and exosomes represent a frontier in regenerative medicine that goes far beyond old political controversy — and women in research are shaping what the next generation of care looks like.

pullQuotes.text: You're not broken. What you're experiencing is real — and it has a name.
The estrogen study that scared women for a generation got it wrong. We deserve better science and better answers.
Women leading the research is how the next generation gets better care.
Self-advocacy is the skill of this season. Get educated, then ask better questions.

quiz.title: What Is Your Perimenopause Power Style?
quiz.description: How you navigate your health tells you a lot about your leadership instincts. Take this quiz to discover your midlife wellness archetype — and find out what it means for how you show up for yourself.

A.name: The Informed Advocate
A.description: You believe that knowledge is power, and when it comes to your health, you come prepared. You research before appointments, ask follow-up questions, and you are not afraid to push back if something doesn't sit right with you. You may sometimes carry the weight of knowing too much without enough support around you — and that is exactly why this episode was made for you. Dr. Mari and Aimee validate every instinct you have had, give you the science to back it up, and remind you that the most powerful thing you can do is keep asking better questions.

B.name: The Quiet Processor
B.description: You tend to internalize what you're experiencing before you talk about it — sometimes so deeply that you wonder if what you're feeling is even real. You are thoughtful, self-aware, and often the last person in the room to ask for help. This episode will feel like a exhale. Hearing two brilliant women name the symptoms you have been quietly carrying — brain fog, not feeling like yourself, the emotional shifts — will give you language for what you have been living, and the courage to finally bring it into the room with your doctor.

C.name: The Resilient Optimizer
C.description: You are a problem-solver at your core, and when your body starts sending signals, your first instinct is to optimize your way through — better sleep habits, cleaner nutrition, more movement. That instinct serves you, and this episode builds directly on it. Dr. Mari's conversation about muscle, bone density, whole foods, and longevity gives your optimizer nature a science-backed foundation, while also inviting you to consider that sometimes the solution isn't just doing more — it's understanding the deeper hormonal landscape beneath the symptoms.

D.name: The Brave Beginner
D.description: You are just starting to pay attention to what midlife might mean for your body, and honestly, some of what you have heard about perimenopause has been confusing or even a little scary. You are in exactly the right place. This episode is designed to meet you where you are — no jargon, no shame, no assumption that you already have all the answers. Dr. Mari and Aimee break down the science in the most human way possible, and you will walk away feeling informed, empowered, and ready to start asking your own questions.

questions.question: You have been feeling off for a few weeks — tired, foggy, a little emotionally raw. What is your first instinct?

options.text: I start researching symptoms online and come to my next doctor's appointment with a list of questions ready.
I sit with it quietly for a while, wondering if it's stress or just a phase, before I mention it to anyone.
I immediately audit my sleep, nutrition, and exercise routine to see what I can tighten up.
I mention it casually to a friend and wait to see if it gets worse before I take any action.

questions.question: A friend tells you her doctor dismissed her concerns about perimenopause symptoms. How do you respond?

options.text: I tell her she absolutely needs to find a new doctor and offer to help her research providers who specialize in hormone health.
I listen and validate her — I have probably had a similar experience and didn't know how to handle it either.
I share every lifestyle change that has helped me and suggest she track her symptoms in detail before the next appointment.
I feel for her but honestly I am not sure what the right advice is — I am still figuring this out myself.

questions.question: When you think about adding strength training to your routine for bone density and longevity, your honest reaction is:

options.text: I already do it and I have read the research — I will not apologize for prioritizing this.
I know I should but something always seems more urgent, and I quietly feel guilty about it.
I am already planning a new training schedule and want to understand the science behind it more deeply.
Honestly, I had not connected exercise to hormonal health before — I am open to learning more.

questions.question: How would you describe your relationship with the medical system right now?

options.text: I see myself as a partner in my care — I come prepared and I expect to be taken seriously.
I tend to defer to my doctor even when something feels off, because I am not sure my instincts are right.
I have a team of providers and I actively manage my health like I manage a project.
I go when something is wrong, but I have not thought much about proactive or preventive care yet.

questions.question: You hear that women were excluded from clinical research until 1993. What is your gut reaction?

options.text: I am furious and it motivates me to be even more proactive about demanding better care.
It makes me feel sad and a little validated — like it explains why I have felt unseen in medical spaces.
It makes me want to find the best current research and apply it to my own health plan immediately.
It is shocking to me — I had no idea, and it makes me want to learn a lot more.

questions.question: When it comes to your health in your forties and beyond, which statement feels most true for you right now?

options.text: I am my own best advocate, and I take that responsibility seriously even when it is exhausting.
I often wonder if what I am experiencing is normal or if I am making too big a deal out of it.
I treat my health like a system to optimize — I am always looking for the next lever to pull.
I am starting to realize I need to pay more attention to this chapter of my health, and I am not sure where to begin.

content:
There is a moment that so many women describe — somewhere in their late thirties or early forties — where they look around at their life and think, something is wrong with me. The sleep is off. The patience is thinner. The words won't come the way they used to. The body feels unfamiliar. And yet, when they bring it up — to a friend, a doctor, sometimes even a partner — the response is a shrug, a referral to a therapist, or the quiet suggestion that maybe they should just slow down.

This episode of The Manage Her® Podcast exists to tell you: you are not broken. What you are experiencing has a name, a mechanism, and a growing body of science behind it. And you deserve to understand all of it.

Aimee Rickabus sits down with Dr. Mari Mitrani — physician-scientist, stem cell researcher, biotech founder, and one of the most compelling voices in regenerative and preventive medicine — for a conversation that goes far past the hot flashes and night sweats most people associate with menopause. Both women are navigating perimenopause in real time, which means this isn't a clinical lecture. It's a real, grounded, honest exchange between two high-performing women who refuse to accept that this transition should be something we suffer through in silence.

The Research Gap That Cost Women Decades

Here's a fact that should make every woman pause: women were not consistently included in clinical research until 1993. Think about that. For most of modern medical history, the default human body in research was male. The implications ripple out across virtually every area of medicine — but they land with particular weight in the conversation about hormones, heart health, and perimenopause. When the science wasn't built with us in mind, the guidance that followed wasn't either. And that gap is something Dr. Mari has dedicated significant energy to closing.

Understanding this context matters not just academically — it matters practically, because it explains why so many women feel dismissed. It explains why the symptoms they describe don't match the checklists their doctors were trained on. And it is precisely why self-advocacy has to become a core competency for any woman navigating midlife health.

The Symptoms No One Warned You About

When most people think of perimenopause, they think of hot flashes. But Dr. Mari walks through the fuller picture, and it is striking how many symptoms get filed under stress, depression, anxiety, or simply aging — never connected to hormonal transition. Brain fog. Joint pain. Heart palpitations. Changes in mood and emotional regulation. The creeping, persistent sense that you no longer feel like yourself. That last one — I don't feel like myself — is now being recognized as a legitimate, documentable symptom, not a vague complaint to be brushed aside.

Dr. Mari and Aimee also explore how ethnicity shapes the perimenopause experience, including how long the transition lasts and how it presents. This is a crucial reminder that there is no single universal template. Your experience is specific to you — your biology, your history, your life — and your care should reflect that specificity.

What the Famous Estrogen Study Actually Got Wrong

The Women's Health Initiative study, published in 2002, sent a shockwave through women's health. Hormone replacement therapy was abruptly stopped for millions of women, and an entire generation absorbed the message that estrogen was dangerous. Fear became the dominant framework. But as Dr. Mari explains with clarity and without condescension, that study had serious methodological problems. The women enrolled were older. The doses were higher. The formulations were different from what personalized, lower-dose hormone care looks like today. The headlines did not reflect the nuance — and women paid the price in quality of life, bone density, heart health, and more.

This is not a conversation that dismisses risk. It is a conversation that insists on accuracy. The right approach to hormone care is individualized, involves a trained provider, and looks nothing like a one-size-fits-all fear response. If you have been avoiding this conversation because of something you read or heard twenty years ago, this episode will give you the updated, honest picture you deserve.

Muscle, Bone, and the Long Game

One of the most actionable threads in this conversation is the emphasis on muscle and bone density as longevity tools — not vanity goals, not optional extras, but foundational investments in how you age. The data on strength training for women in midlife is compelling, and Dr. Mari makes the case clearly: what you build in your forties has direct bearing on your independence, your mobility, and your vitality in your seventies and beyond. Movement is medicine, and this conversation gives you the science to back up what your body may already be asking for.

The Frontier: Stem Cells, Exosomes, and What's Coming

For the genuinely curious, Dr. Mari takes a portion of this conversation into the territory of regenerative medicine — explaining stem cells and exosomes in plain language, without jargon, and without the political baggage that has historically surrounded the topic. This is the frontier of what is possible for women's health and longevity, and hearing it explained by someone who has spent her career at the intersection of science and advocacy is genuinely exciting. The conversation closes with a reflection on legacy — the idea that women leading research today are shaping what the next generation inherits. That is not a small thing.

You Are Allowed to Ask Better Questions

Perhaps the most important thread woven throughout this entire episode is permission — permission to take up space in the doctor's office, to change providers if yours isn't listening, to track your symptoms starting at 35 even if no one told you to, to invest in understanding your own body. Self-advocacy is not aggression. It is not being difficult. It is what this moment in your life requires. And this conversation, from two women who are living it out loud, is one of the most powerful invitations to step into that advocacy that we have ever aired on this podcast.

You are not broken. You are in a transition. And you are not navigating it alone.
